steps.title: Identify the sentence parts
steps.content: The sentence first says that scholars wishing to read a work by Varro needn't despair. It then gives the reason: they can still obtain a copy of Rerum rusticarum libri tres. The punctuation at the blank has to show how though relates to the first clause and how the next clause begins.

steps.title: Compare the punctuation
steps.content: Choice A makes though they can still procure read like a subordinate clause, which wrongly suggests that access to the book is a contrast rather than the reason not to despair. Choice B sets off though with commas and then leaves only a comma before the next independent clause. Choice D uses the dash to make though introduce a contrast with the second clause instead of attaching to needn't despair.

steps.title: Use the needed boundary
steps.content: The sentence needs a comma to attach though to needn't despair, followed by a semicolon to separate the two independent clauses. That punctuation preserves the intended meaning and creates a valid boundary before they can still procure. The answer is C.
